questions.question: Explain the concept of partial failure in distributed systems. Why is it more challenging to handle than complete failure?
keyPoints: Partial failure occurs when some components fail while others continue operating
Difficult to detect which components have failed
Can lead to inconsistent system state
Requires sophisticated recovery and reconciliation mechanisms
Harder to diagnose than complete failure which is obvious
System may appear to work partially but produce incorrect results

questions.question: Discuss three security challenges specific to distributed systems that do not exist in centralized systems.
keyPoints: Network vulnerability: data exposed during transmission
Lack of perimeter: no clear boundary to secure
Authentication complexity across multiple trust domains
Authorization in heterogeneous environments
Key management complexity across many nodes
Potential for man-in-the-middle attacks at various points

questions.question: What is the CAP theorem? A banking system chooses Consistency over Availability. Explain the implications during a network partition.
keyPoints: CAP theorem: can provide only 2 of 3 - Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
During partition, must choose between C and A
Choosing Consistency: system blocks/unavailable during partition
Transactions fail or queue until partition resolves
No inconsistent data possible during partition
Trade-off: unavailability for correctness

questions.question: Explain the 'blocking' problem in Two-Phase Commit protocol. Under what conditions does it occur?
keyPoints: 2PC has two phases: Prepare and Commit
After voting 'Yes', participants hold locks and wait for decision
If coordinator crashes after Prepare phase, participants block indefinitely
No way to unilaterally commit or rollback
Requires manual intervention to resolve
3PC attempts to solve this but makes other assumptions

questions.question: Describe the different types of transparency in distributed systems with an example for each.
keyPoints: Access transparency: hide differences in data access methods
Location transparency: access resources without knowing their location
Migration transparency: resources can move without user knowledge
Relocation transparency: resource moves while in use
Replication transparency: multiple copies exist but hidden
Concurrency transparency: multiple users share resources without conflict
Failure transparency: system continues despite component failures

questions.question: Why is clock synchronization problematic in distributed systems? Explain with reference to clock skew and drift.
keyPoints: No global clock exists in distributed systems
Physical clocks run at slightly different rates (clock drift)
Network latency affects synchronization messages (clock skew)
True synchronization is impossible due to message delays
Logical clocks (Lamport, Vector) used as alternative
Algorithms depend on synchronized time for event ordering

questions.question: Explain the 'Fallacies of Distributed Computing' and their practical implications.
keyPoints: Network is reliable - but networks fail frequently
Latency is zero - significant delays over wide areas
Bandwidth is infinite - limited and expensive
Network is secure - many attack vectors
Topology doesn't change - dynamic environments
There is one administrator - multiple organizations
Transport cost is zero - significant overhead

questions.question: A distributed system designer must choose between strong consistency (CP) and high availability (AP) during network partitions. Discuss the trade-offs involved with specific application examples. When would you recommend each approach?
keyPoints: Strong consistency (CP): Banking, inventory systems - correctness critical; transactions blocked during partition; data never inconsistent; example: Google Spanner, ZooKeeper
High availability (AP): Social media, caching, CDNs; always serve requests; eventual consistency; examples: Cassandra, Dynamo, Amazon shopping cart
Trade-offs: performance, latency, fault tolerance vs correctness
Hybrid approaches: Read your own writes, causal consistency
Application requirements determine choice
Modern systems often provide tunable consistency
CAP theorem: partition is not optional, choose CA or AP during design

questions.question: Design a distributed deadlock detection algorithm for a system with multiple sites. Explain how you would handle the challenges of false cycles and communication overhead.
keyPoints: Centralized: one site collects all wait-for information; simple but single point of failure
Hierarchical: organize sites in tree; reduces communication; compromise between centralized and fully distributed
Fully distributed: sites exchange information; complex but robust
False cycles: due to propagation delays, detected cycles may no longer exist; use timestamps, periodic verification
Optimization: only send changes, compress information, use probes
Resolution: choose victim, rollback, compensate
Challenge: concurrency makes detection non-trivial
Trade-off: detection accuracy vs communication cost

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Three-Phase Commit protocol with Two-Phase Commit. Why doesn't Three-Phase Commit completely solve the commit problem in all failure scenarios?
keyPoints: 2PC: Prepare, Commit/Rollback phases; blocking if coordinator fails after prepare
3PC adds 'Pre-commit' phase between Prepare and Commit
3PC ensures no blocking if coordinator fails (can timeout and commit)
Assumes synchronous communication: messages eventually delivered
Network partition during Pre-commit can still cause issues
Cannot handle Byzantine failures
Still has single point of failure in coordinator
More complex, more messages, more latency
In practice, Paxos/Raft preferred for consensus
3PC assumes failures are fail-stop, not arbitrary
